title: Narrow Painter's Ladder Shelf
summary: A leaning ladder shelf with graduated shelves — deep at the bottom, shallow at the top. The 15° lean is what makes the shallow parts work.
description:
A narrow leaning shelf shaped like a painter's ladder: four shelves that step from deep at the bottom to shallow at the top, on legs canted back at 15° so the whole thing rests against a wall. It's a classic bathroom or entry piece — the tapering shelves fit the leaning geometry, and the narrow footprint tucks into tight spots.

The lean is the whole idea. The front legs are cut at 15° off square and the top back corners squared off so the ladder stands at a consistent rake against the wall. Because it leans, the joints carry very little load — most connections are just a couple of brad nails and glue, or pocket screws from the back if you'd rather. Don't over-engineer it.

Build the two side ladders first, tie them together at the back, then build and drop in the four graduated shelves. The shelves are simple trays: a 1x12 bottom cut to each shelf's depth (4-3/4", 8-1/2", 12-1/4", 16") with 1x3 trim around the front and sides. Measure and cut the front trim to fit the actual assembled width — it should come out around 12-3/4" — rather than trusting the number, since the leg spacing sets it.

tools.note: The 15° leg cuts and the shelf trim.
Squaring the top back corners of the front legs.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Shelf fronts, backs, and side trim. (Source shopping list says 1x4; the cut list uses 1x3.)

cutList.note: Ends cut 15° off square; square the top back corner with a circular saw.
Measure and cut to the actual assembled width (~12-3/4").
Same length as the shelf trim.

steps.title: Cut the legs
steps.body: Cut the legs per the cut list. For the front legs, also square up the top back corners with a circular saw so the ladder meets the wall cleanly at its 15° rake. The front legs are longer than the backs because of the lean — don't mix them up.

steps.title: Join front and back legs
steps.body: Attach each front leg to a back leg. This is not a structural joint — a couple of 2" nails or screws from the back with glue is plenty. Pocket holes work too, but keep them on the back side so they don't show. Build both side ladders the same.

steps.title: Connect the two ladders
steps.body: Join the two side ladders together at the back with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue, or pocket holes from the back side. This sets the overall width, which in turn sets your shelf-trim length — measure it now before cutting the front trim.

steps.title: Build the shelf trays
steps.body: Trim each shelf with 1x3: attach the side trim first with 1-1/4" brad nails and glue, then measure and cut the front and back trim (about 12-3/4") to fit and attach to the front and back of every shelf. The graduated depths — 4-3/4", 8-1/2", 12-1/4", 16" — are what fit the ladder's taper, so keep them in order.

steps.title: Attach the shelves and finish
steps.body: Attach the shelves to the sides with glue and 1-1/4" brad nails — you can nail from outside the legs if that's easier. Drive the 2" trim-head screws where you want extra hold. Sand and finish. Because it leans, tip it against the wall and check it sits stable before loading it.

images.alt: Narrow leaning ladder shelf with graduated shelves deep at the bottom
